paper_title: Online Multiscale Dynamic Topic Models

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: Finding a suitable research paper matching all Part A constraints
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: Part A requires selecting one classical machine learning research paper under given constraints. The paper must be published between 2009-2012, from a top-tier A* conference (NeurIPS, ICML, AISTATS, KDD), whose primary contribution is a classical method in SVM, GMM, or Time Series modeling. Give me some paper suggestions related to my project: Dynamic Trend & Event Detector in Social Media Analytics.
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: Claude suggested multiple candidate papers including Online LDA (NeurIPS 2010) and Online Multiscale Dynamic Topic Models (KDD 2010), with a detailed comparison of each against the constraints.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: I verified both papers on ACM Digital Library and confirmed their publication venues, years, and that their primary contributions are methodological and classical ML-based.

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: Comparing two candidate papers to decide which is better for the project
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: Now tell which is best: Online Learning for Latent Dirichlet Allocation or Online Multiscale Dynamic Topic Models
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: Claude provided a structured comparison table covering venue prestige, project fit, reproducibility, Part C explainability, and award recognition. This helped me make an informed final decision.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: I cross-checked that KDD is indeed a CORE A* venue by visiting https://portal.core.edu.au and confirmed MDTM won Best Paper Honorable Mention at KDD 2010.

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: Verifying the correct official link for the selected paper
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: https://www.researchgate.net/publication/221653561_Online_Multiscale_Dynamic_Topic_Models — is this the link?
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: Claude confirmed the paper identity and provided the correct official ACM Digital Library link instead of ResearchGate, which requires login.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: I visited https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/1835804.1835889 and confirmed the paper title, authors, year, and conference match exactly.

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: Checking whether the selected paper satisfies all official Part A constraints from the exam document
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: This is the final doc for Part A — now tell the paper we selected for this is correct or not. The paper we selected does not have an arXiv paper.
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: Claude ran a systematic constraint-by-constraint check against the official exam rules and confirmed that the absence of an arXiv version is acceptable and even preferred since the paper is published only through official ACM proceedings.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: I re-read the exam document constraint: arXiv-only papers are banned, not papers without arXiv. This matched Claude's explanation. Verified the ACM link is the official publisher version.

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: Understanding the core methodology of the paper and why it qualifies as GMM and Variant
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: Tell the core methodology — SVM and Variant / Time Series and Variant / GMM and Variant
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: Claude explained that LDA and its variants are Dirichlet-based mixture models analogous to GMMs, using Stochastic EM inference — the same algorithmic family as GMM fitting. This clarified why GMM and Variant is the correct category.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: I verified this by reading the paper's generative model section, which uses Dirichlet priors and EM-based inference, confirming the GMM family classification.

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: Understanding my own project description so I can connect it to the selected paper
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: Okay now can you explain me my project to me
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: Claude broke down all 4 layers of the project (Baseline ML, Advanced ML, DL, Hybrid) and showed exactly how the MDTM paper fits into the Advanced ML tier and enables dynamic trend and event detection.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: I matched Claude's explanation against the official project description document and confirmed all layers and objectives were correctly interpreted.

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: Getting help filling in the Google Form submission fields accurately
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: Timestamp, Full Name, Authors, Year of Publication, Link to the Paper, Primary Method Used, Roll Number, Paper Title, Email ID, Conference/Journal Name, Is the primary contribution methodological, Does the paper use publicly accessible data, Does the paper require GPU/restricted data, reproducibility responsibility, checked live sheet, duplicate disqualification, Why did you choose this paper — these are the things I have to add, can you help me to do this
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: Claude populated all form fields with accurate information and provided a well-reasoned answer for the 'Why did you choose this paper' field that I reviewed and verified before using.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: I reviewed every field against the paper's ACM page and the exam document to ensure all information is accurate before submission.

top_5_prompts.prompt: Now tell which is best: Online Learning for Latent Dirichlet Allocation or Online Multiscale Dynamic Topic Models
top_5_prompts.why_important: This was the most critical decision point — choosing between two valid papers. The structured comparison made me think about trade-offs between venue prestige, reproducibility difficulty, project alignment, and explainability in Part C. It helped me reason about what matters most for a complete 3-part exam, not just Part A.

top_5_prompts.prompt: This is the final doc for Part A — now tell the paper we selected for this is correct or not. The paper we selected does not have an arXiv paper.
top_5_prompts.why_important: This prompt was analytically important because I was concerned that having no arXiv version might disqualify the paper. Understanding the distinction between arXiv-only papers (banned) versus papers without arXiv (perfectly fine) was a key insight that gave me confidence to proceed with submission.

top_5_prompts.prompt: Tell the core methodology — SVM and Variant / Time Series and Variant / GMM and Variant
top_5_prompts.why_important: Classifying the paper's methodology correctly is worth marks in the form and sets the foundation for Parts B and C. Understanding why Dirichlet-based topic models belong to the GMM family (shared EM inference, mixture model structure) deepened my understanding of the paper's algorithmic core.

top_5_prompts.prompt: Part A requires selecting one classical machine learning research paper under given constraints. The paper must be published between 2009-2012, from a top-tier A* conference (NeurIPS, ICML, AISTATS, KDD), whose primary contribution is a classical method in SVM, GMM, or Time Series modeling. Give me some paper suggestions related to my project: Dynamic Trend & Event Detector in Social Media Analytics.
top_5_prompts.why_important: This was the starting point of my paper selection process. It helped me understand what kinds of papers satisfy all constraints simultaneously, and introduced me to the MDTM paper which I would not have found easily on my own given the specific intersection of GMM methods and temporal topic modeling.

top_5_prompts.prompt: Okay now can you explain me my project to me
top_5_prompts.why_important: Understanding how my own project connects to the selected paper was essential for writing a strong justification in the form and for future Parts B and C. This prompt helped me see that MDTM directly implements the Advanced ML tier of my project and that its multiscale short-timescale component naturally maps to breaking event detection.

full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: Suggest an SVM paper published between 2008-2013 in a top-tier venue (ECCV, ICML, NeurIPS) that introduces a core methodological contribution regarding kernel optimization.
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: Identified 'A Fast Dual Method for HIK SVM Learning' (ECCV 2010) as a significant methodological contribution to dual-space SVM solvers.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: Verified publication venue (ECCV 2010) and confirmed the paper satisfies the 2008-2013 temporal constraint[cite: 3].

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: Understanding methodological novelty
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: What is the core methodological contribution of the ICD solver and how does it optimize the Histogram Intersection Kernel (HIK)?
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: Clarified that the Inverse Coordinate Descent (ICD) is a deterministic dual-space solver that utilizes the additive property of HIK to achieve high scalability[cite: 3].
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: Reviewed Section 3 of the paper to confirm the derivation of the dual objective and the deterministic nature of the ICD algorithm[cite: 3].

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: Theoretical validation of the kernel

full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: Confirmed the paper provides a formal proof that HIK is a positive definite kernel for non-negative real-valued feature vectors[cite: 3].
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: Cross-checked the proof in Section 2 and confirmed its mathematical alignment with Mercer's condition[cite: 3].

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: Empirical robustness and parameter sensitivity

full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: Highlighted the finding that ICD is not sensitive to the C parameter, allowing high accuracy with default settings[cite: 3].
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: Verified the experimental results in Section 4 using datasets such as DaimlerChrysler and INRIA Person[cite: 3].

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: Understanding the scope and constraints of Part A before selecting a paper
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: Help me select the research paper for the part A of ML midsem
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: Claude explained the key selection constraints — CORE A* venue, 2009–2012, SVM/GMM/ARIMA methods, CPU-only reproducibility — and gave initial candidates including Vedaldi & Zisserman CVPR 2010.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: I cross-checked the CORE A* venue list at portal.core.edu.au to confirm NIPS and CVPR qualify. I also verified the year constraint by re-reading the exam PDF.

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: Filtering out already-taken papers and finding a unique paper
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: All these are taken by other students so find me the unique research paper within the given timeline of 2009 to 2012 (inclusive) [provided list of ~80 taken papers]
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: Claude identified 'Nyström Method vs Random Fourier Features' (Yang et al., NIPS 2012) as a strong, unique option not present in the taken list. Also suggested 'A Kernel Two-Sample Test' as backup.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: I manually scanned the entire taken-papers list line by line to confirm 'Nyström Method vs Random Fourier Features' was not present. I also searched the live response sheet to double-check.

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: Assessing reproducibility and compute feasibility of the Nyström paper
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: Find me the easiest research and short paper for the above
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: Claude confirmed that the Nyström paper is directly reproducible via sklearn's built-in Nystroem and RBFSampler classes, requiring zero GPU and running on any laptop. Pointed out that the paper is 9 pages and has clear failure conditions.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: I verified by running 'from sklearn.kernel_approximation import Nystroem, RBFSampler' locally — it worked without any additional installs. I also confirmed the USPS and GISETTE datasets are publicly available.

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: Understanding the core technical contribution of the Nyström paper
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: Explain the core difference between Nyström method and Random Fourier Features for kernel approximation, and when each one fails
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: Claude explained that Nyström approximates the kernel matrix by sampling actual data points as landmarks, while RFF uses random projections from the Fourier transform of the kernel. Nyström wins when the kernel matrix has fast-decaying eigenvalues; RFF wins when the budget is large and the spectrum is flat.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: I read Sections 2 and 3 of the paper and verified this description matches the theoretical analysis in Theorems 1 and 2. The eigenspectrum argument is explicitly stated in the paper.

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: Drafting the Google Form submission and JSON file for LLM usage disclosure
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: Help me fill the form and draft the complete llm_usage_partA.json
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: Claude pre-filled all Google Form fields and generated the full JSON structure including interaction log, top-5 prompts, and student declaration — following the exact schema prescribed in the exam PDF.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: I compared every field in the JSON against the required schema in the exam PDF (page 5–7) to ensure correct structure, field names, and data types. I also reviewed all interaction entries for accuracy.

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: Understanding failure modes of the Nyström method for Part C preparation
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: What are the failure cases of the Nyström kernel approximation method and in what situations does Random Fourier Features outperform it?
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: Claude explained three main failure conditions: (1) when landmark points are sampled poorly or don't represent the data distribution, (2) when the approximation rank is too small relative to dataset complexity, (3) when the eigenspectrum of the kernel matrix decays slowly. In these cases RFF's data-independent approximation is more stable.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: I verified condition (3) against the paper's Theorem 1 which explicitly bounds the approximation error in terms of eigenvalue decay. Conditions (1) and (2) are discussed in the paper's experimental section.

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: Understanding the connection between kernel approximation and SVM training to confirm method alignment with exam constraints
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: How does the Nyström method connect to Support Vector Machines? Does this paper count as an SVM paper?
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: Claude clarified that the Nyström and RFF approximations are used specifically to make kernel SVMs tractable — the approximate feature map replaces the explicit kernel evaluation in SVM training, allowing a linear SVM to approximate a nonlinear kernel SVM at much lower cost. This confirmed the paper fits the 'SVM / kernel methods / variants' criterion.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: Verified by reading the paper's introduction which explicitly states the goal is scalable kernel SVM training. The experimental section trains SVMs on the approximate feature maps.

top_5_prompts.prompt: All these are taken by other students so find me the unique research paper within the given timeline of 2009 to 2012 (inclusive)

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: Methodological Verification
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: Tell me more about Adaptive Regularization of Weight Vectors (AROW). Does it actually count as an SVM variant for my assignment? What exactly did they introduce that is new compared to standard Passive-Aggressive algorithms?
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: Clarified that AROW is a large-margin online learning algorithm that extends margin-based methods using second-order confidence information, differentiating it from first-order PA algorithms.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: Read the introduction and comparison sections of the paper to confirm that AROW is positioned as a robust margin-based learner and closely related to online SVM-style methods.

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: Mathematical Understanding

full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: Explained that the covariance matrix encodes uncertainty about each parameter and enables adaptive regularization, especially useful in noisy environments.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: Re-read the section describing the Gaussian distribution over weights and connected it to the update rule to confirm the confidence interpretation.

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: Equation-Level Clarity

full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: Clarified that AROW minimizes a regularized loss with a KL-divergence constraint between Gaussian weight distributions rather than solving a quadratic program.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: Reviewed the optimization objective in the paper and verified that it includes KL divergence and hinge-like loss terms.

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: Feasibility & Implementation Logic

full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: Examined the equations and confirmed they are based on matrix-vector multiplications and rank-one updates.

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: Failure Mode Analysis

full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: Checked experimental section and compared reported performance against kernel-based methods to understand its limitations.

full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: Claude searched across multiple CORE A* venues and identified 'Beyond Blacklists: Learning to Detect Malicious Web Sites from Suspicious URLs' (Ma et al., KDD 2009) as a strong candidate. The paper applies SVM to detect malicious URLs — an intuitive binary classification task with a public dataset on UCI ML Repository.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: Verified the paper exists on ACM Digital Library (DOI: 10.1145/1557019.1557153). Confirmed the UCI dataset is available at archive.ics.uci.edu/dataset/187/url+reputation. Read the paper abstract to confirm SVM is the primary method.

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: Understanding the core methodology of the selected paper

full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: Clarified that the paper extracts two types of features from URLs — lexical features (URL length, number of dots, presence of IP address, suspicious tokens) and host-based features (WHOIS age, geographic info, IP block). full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: Read Sections 3 and 4 of the paper to verify the feature extraction process and SVM setup. The explanation aligned with the paper's description of lexical and host-based feature sets.

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: Identifying limitations and failure cases of the paper's approach

full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: Cross-checked with Section 6 of the paper where the authors discuss the adversarial nature of the problem and the need for online learning to adapt to evolving threats.
